speaker: interviewer
text: It's June the 13th, 1996. The survivor's name is Steven-- Steven Pearl. The interviewer is Bernice Krantz. The city is London. The country is England. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Bernice Krantz. It's June the 13th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Steven Pearl, and we're in London, England. Can you please give me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Steven Pearl, formerly Solomon Gantz. Solomon was S-O-L-O-M-O-N, which is-- was easy for me to spell when I was very young. And Gantz is G-A-N-T-Z. We were-- when I got naturalized, I changed my name to Steven Pearl, which was our surname from home, which is spelled S-T-E-V-E-N. P-E-A-R-L, surname.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you please give me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My date of birth is 28 of September, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age--

speaker: interviewee
text: 26, I'm sorry. 26 of September, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and the country you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a little village called Cuhea, which is in Marmarosch. The capital of Marmarosch was Siget, and that is in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell this name of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Siget. S-I-G-E-T. That was the capital of our [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: County.

speaker: interviewee
text: County. Couldn't think of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] If we could start by talking about your early childhood, your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. Well, I remember when I was very young, we were the same as all the kids, Jewish kids, in Europe. I used to have to get up in the morning very early, go to cheder, to daven. Took about 45 minutes. I didn't go home from there. I had to go straight to school. School was from 9:00 till 1:00. And in then back to cheder till about 5:00 or 5:30 in the evening. So I was virtually away all day from my parents, and all that. And that's how it was for everybody, not just for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: From four or five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right up-- right up to your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can Can you tell me your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was David Perl. It was without the A. I added the A when I got naturalized.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was what I would call today-- we had a kosher butcher shop. But he was also a dogsbody. Like my father was one of these people who could lay his hands onto anything. We laugh at it now. When somebody or another had a toothache, they used to call my father. He had a pair of pliers which hardly met, but somehow he managed to pull their teeth out. And if not with the pliers, it was to shut the door with the string. And then they used to help delivering calves and all that. I don't know where he learned, how he learned. But seemed to be-- he seemed to have this knowledge. And they used to-- in fact, at the time when they were talking about, you know, taking people away for work to-- for labor into Germany, we were kidding ourselves thinking that we will not be touched because my father was wanted to help out in the village and all the rest of it. You name it, he did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Where did he come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in the next village, which was called [ROMANIAN]. I believe. But I'm not 100% sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Chaia Gantz. And she still had a father who lived in Viseu, which was quite a big town. And he remarried. And he-- her father had a son which was the-- almost the same age as my mother-- as my younger brother, which sort of-- it sounds funny. But that's how it was. He remarried, and he had this boy with his new wife of his. Yeah. And from there, we used to go on our summer holidays, like to go and stay with them because they had a shop, sweet shop. We used to love it, of course, as kids. A big garden with apples trees, and pear trees, and we-- you-- sort of you name it. And we used to spend three or four weeks with-- which is, of course, was my zaide. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three sisters and we were three sisters and three brothers. And they were saying that the-- I had three sisters on the trot, and they were going bananas because no boys. And then after that, three boys were born. My brother is the oldest who lives-- at the moment, he lives in [NON-ENGLISH] with number two. And the third brother, which was called Yontle. He was only 11. And he was with us together in the Auschwitz, and they picked him out and said he's too young for work. He's going to his mother. That's the last I saw of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was life like in your house before--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: --hard-- hard to imagine, hard to believe what it was like. I mean, they used to do their own baking and their own everything. We had sort of two bedrooms and a big living room. It was a great big oven, sort of-- it was built from brick where they had hole in to cook. Saucepans. There was always three or four saucepans on that. And next to it, there was an oven which they baked their own bread and pretzels and all that. And we used to come home from school or cheder. We used to go up on what we call on-- on the pitcher, which was very, very warm. Because when the-- when they use the oven, we come home freezing. We used to go out there and have a good warm up. Yeah. It was-- hard-- it's hard to explain, hard to imagine. But that's how it was. Used to have snows six feet. It-- it was no problem in-- in Romania where we lived. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a good time with your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very. Because we were all mucking in almost like with friends. I mean, everybody was very, very close to one another, sisters and brothers. I don't remember ever, ever having a fight with any of them. All that. And it was-- I think life was obviously much different to what it is today. We had-- as I said, we had two bedrooms. Mom-- Mom and Dad had one. And we had straw in our beds, and we used to change the straw every month or so. And just put sort of a sheet over the straw. And sometimes it stuck you, and all that. And-- and that's how it was. We didn't have mattresses, or we didn't have the silk, what they have today, the-- the comfort and all that. We used to have pillows made from our own geese, feathers, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you all sleep in one bed, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was-- the boys slept in one bed and the girls in another in the same room. You know, there was-- all-- everybody was mucking in. There-- there was no shyness, or didn't think about things like that. And it was-- it was a nice life in one way.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then when you think about it today, can be how could we have done it? How was it possible? You know, today I think they'd lock you up if you slept-- elder sisters and brothers sort of sleeping in one room, and all the rest of it. Almost, anyhow. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not fanatically religious. My father sort of always living was kosher, and we carried on the same as everybody else in the village. Some of them were more religious than others. They used to go to shul every morning, and daven, and then evening for maariv and all that. But I don't remember sort of-- I mean, the kids make having to go to until they were 13. And then obviously it was the same for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you kept Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, more than sure, and how. Used to bake our own challah and used to-- I remember they used to take the cholent to one place across the road where about 10 or 12 people used to take their pots and-- pots for-- with this cholent in it. They used to go over the road. And Saturday, when we come back from shul, and bring it home. And looking forward to that, it was like-- oh, like today looking forward to go to America or somewhere. It was nice. We only had white bread on-- on Shabbat. All the rest of the time, we had bread-- black bread, or made from maize. When-- we had a lot of food was maize, which we used to call a-- a certain pudding, it was. But it was almost a main meal. It was called [ROMANIAN]. And we used to make it. Either you used to have the first sort of portion was milk. And then the second portion, it was like they make today, macaroni and cheese. And that's how we used to have that, made from maize. And it was-- it was long strings. It was like hair, but it wasn't. It was the Bryndza. Which was very, very nice at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: My sister sometimes makes it for me when I go to Israel. Yeah. She says, it's good? Takes you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Shlomo, do you like my [ROMANIAN], my sister says.

speaker: interviewer
text: Takes you back a few years, does it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It does. It does. it seems like today. I mean, it's a long time ago. It seems like yesterday in some ways. In other ways, it seems like two centuries ago. Sort of-- but we went back. It was-- I went back with my wife and my friend, Jack and Maureen. And we thought at the time-- I'm talking about still on the communism-- hardly ever-- hardly anything changed. It was almost as we left it 35, 38 years earlier. Nothing has changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same houses. The same horse and carts. And-- unbelievable. Yeah. Like it was stuck in a time warp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like it was stuck in a time warp. Some of them-- there was one biggish town. It was called [ROMANIAN]. And we were saying, They could have filmed here It was so amazing, really. You know, when you come down from-- we came down the hill to this town. And it was set in front of us. And it was just like really. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandparents live nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my grandparents lived in Viseu, as I said. There was a-- there a sweet shop and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about the other set?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- my grandmother died before I was born. And-- but that's why my grandfather remarried. My other grandparents died before I-- before-- a long time. Because my older brother, who was two and a half years older than me, he's named after my other grandfather. So I wouldn't know about them much. And we didn't have photos like today, just shoot and there you are, sort of, you know. So I don't really remember him. And to tell you the truth, I don't even remember my mother because we-- no way have we been able to find a photo of her. We used to send-- we used to send photos to America to my cousins, and I had an aunt who lived in America. Apparently when the auntie died, they made a bonfire in the garden and burned everything, and it-- breaks my heart when I think about it. Because we used to make a whole song and dance about going-- getting the photograph with the camera, take a film-- a photo of the family. And in the end, it was-- it seems to be like a waste because they burned them all. They burned all these photos. They couldn't keep anything. They didn't think about it. And it's a shame. And we never-- none of us-- there's four of us. None of us have got a photo of my mother. We've got photos of my father, luckily. And not my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a shame.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a shame.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your early days at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- quite a lot of things have disappeared from my head. I can't remember. But other silly things I remember. I remember be-- being Jewish, we were-- I may-- if I may say so, we were a little bit cleverer than the rest of them. And we used to-- always used to ask-- the teachers used to ask questions. We always put our hands up. End of the day, she said, right, you lot at the back. Because you're always answering the questions. So we'd rather you leave it to others. And-- but it was all friendly and nice. We-- I went to about three, three and a half years to Romanian classes, then the Hungarians marched in 1941. And we went then for about two, two and a half years, Hungarians classes. When the Hungarians came in, they tried to bash the Romanian out of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you are-- now this is Hungary. And now you're learning Hungarian. And so on and so on. So really, I mean, what was Hebrew, cheder, and shul, daven in. What was Romanian? What was Hungarian? I've forgot the whole lot of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only-- I was only about 14 when I-- when we were taken away to the camps. So you imagine a young boy-- I mean, I know they say the-- the younger you are the more you can learn. But don't-- don't really-- now-- I used to be able to speak Romanian, obviously, and Hungarian. And Yiddish. That's about the only language I can speak properly, Yiddish. And we forgot it. My brother and his wife, they were both Romanian. And they speak-- they used to speak before they could speak [? Ivrit, ?] they spoke Romanian. Now they speak Romanian. They speak Hungarian to one another. Me, I'm a [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you didn't have anybody to speak to.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm so sorry I didn't have a book. If I had a-- had a book from the library and just read one page a month of two or three languages, Hungarian, Romanian, and Russian, Polish. I understood them all. And just disappeared. I mean, you pick up-- lucky enough to pick up English. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go-- you didn't go to a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was cheder. No Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just cheder. No. It was mixed classes. Mixed class. I remember one year, we were all getting busy and all spick and span, polished up, dressed properly. Because Prince Michael was coming to about half a dozen villages to visit the schools. I mean, today-- then he became king, and now-- I mean, now, I don't see think they wouldn't let him in. I think he lives in Greece or somewhere. And I remember like-- things like that which stuck out in my mind. I mean, Prince Michael in a car. We only saw a car once a year, or if-- if that. And it was something different with flags and God knows what else, yeah. It was really something. I mean, little things like that would stick out in my mind, because it was just one of those things which happened once in a lifetime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little. It was mostly when the people used to get out of the-- leave the pubs and all that. And sometimes they used to throw stones at Jewish houses, and all that. There was one particular evening where I think they were more or less told it was a mitzvah to throw a stone at Jewish houses. That was Easter. And we had very-- we were very friendly with all our neighbors. In fact, with all the village. Everybody knew everybody else. But I swear to you, my father caught our neighbor picking up a pebble and throwing at the house. Because that's how it was. I mean, she wasn't meaning to do any damage. But to her, she got-- they come out at midnight mass. And that's what they were-- we used to-- we had special blinds where we used to put up only once a year. And that was just that time, for that-- that particular night when they used to come home from church at-- after midnight mass or whatever, and they used to-- as I said, even the very, very, very good ones used to chuck stones at Jewish houses. They were incited in some way. They were told it's a mitzvah to them, and they did it. Yeah. And as I said, that's the only time-- that's the only time in-- in the whole year we used to have to put up these blinds. So we don't get broken windows and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your father have to stop working at any point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. He sort of worked right through. He was also dealing-- he was buying cattle. Not many, not like what they thinking today about hundreds. Two or three cattle, he was buying. And he was selling in the next village. And he was making a few-- a few bob that way. And probably a couple of horses. And he was wheeling and dealing. And he was a very adaptable person. He's-- as-- we're lucky we have got a photo of him, in our-- and you can see it. He'll show it to you later, and you can see. He's a very tall man. A nice man. And funny enough, my sister and my brother sort of take after him because they are both tall. And I've got another sister and myself, and we take after our mum, and we are both short, sort of, you know. So dark complexion, as you can see me now. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I've only got to sit out in the garden half hour, and everybody says, where you been? Where you been? Yeah. Yeah. That's good. So school was fairly happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think it was happy. Because you see, the-- really it must have been. Because we were taught, and we were that little bit more up with it. And there was no problem about homework or about ever getting cane, because we didn't know the answers and all that. Because really, it was probably I should have been in higher classes and all that. But then it--it just went up to 11. You was in that-- that grade. And from 11, you went up no matter how clever you was, or how-- or not. That's how it worked at the time. I believe it still works here now. 11 classes and all the rest of it. But we didn't have such a thing, 11 plus and all that. It was just your age. You became a certain age, and you went into the next class. The higher grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have a bar mitzvah before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- just-- I just had a bar mitzvah. My bar mitzvah was in September. Obviously it was in sukkah. And I remember them telling me that-- that it was pouring with rain, and they had to ask the rabbi permission to shut the roof of the sukkah, because we had a room in our house. And it was just less than four months later, that we rounded up and taken to the ghetto, which was a large village. About six or seven villages were taken to this ghetto which-- which was called Dragomiresti. And we were put up in school. And we were a-- sort of-- you know, first we put up in-- with families, sort of relatives or friends or whatever. It didn't matter if there was 18 in the house, or-- where there was room for 18, all the families they mucked in. And then gradually, they-- before-- few days before they took us off, we were taken to school buildings. The men only. The women were left at home. And they sort of organized themselves. And they said, right. Everybody carry whatever they can carry. By the morning, we're leaving. You're going to work in Germany, because all the German forces are fighting in the war, and they need labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember how you were rounded up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not rounded up. We were just told. You got to go to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone came to the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think it was posted on trees and things. They just said all Jews have to report to Dragomiresti. Find their own way, where-- who to stay with. In other words, nobody told you who to go to. But everybody had families in that village. And we stayed with a family for about four or five weeks. And then it was sort of right, all men at the school. And we were there about 24, 36 hours. And then there was-- brought the women. They-- I think-- I think they gave you a couple of hours to go and pack and get things ready, and take whatever you can carry with you. To-- you're going to the trains, to Viseu. And then they'll take you off to-- to work in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were rounded up, was it just the men who went to be in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the men were not-- no. There was no round up as such, that-- that we were just-- we did what we were told. All men report at the school house. And I think the school was the only building which had two floors. And there was room for-- for all the men in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would you say were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't really say. About 300, 400. 350, 400.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very crowded. Very-- and nobody knew what was happening. And then again, we was-- they're saying, well, they want us for work. We're going to work, and all that. Nobody knew nothing. Well, at least in the villages they didn't know. But I'll tell you what did happen. Before this, they picked off about eight or 10 which I call hierarchies. The people from the village who supposed to have run the village. And they took them off to Siget. And they held them there. They asked for ransom to let them go. So they collected certain amount of monies to let these people go. And the idea was that if you wanted to buy your freedom or get somebody to help you out, you should be penniless. But then I don't even remember whether they-- they joined their families in the end or not. I don't really remember. I couldn't say. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your brothers went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was-- one of-- one of my sisters lived with an auntie a few villages away. So she wasn't with us. But it was all the family. My-- my mother and-- my mom and dad, my sister Fage and Edith. Etus was the one who lived with another family. Then there was my brother, Chaim, and myself, Shlomo, and then Josef Yontle, which he was five years younger than me, or thereabouts. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then they-- they sort of-- they marched us out together. Families joined up their women folk. And we were taken-- must have marched about three or four hours. There was ordinary-- what they had, they had these students who were training with make believe guns. They looked like guns, but they were only made from wood. And we had about three Germans, and about half a dozen Hungarians. And the rest were just civilian 18, 20-year-olds. And they were sort of stretched out on this long, long queue of people which was six or seven villages. And we were marched to Viseu where we boarded a train all the way to-- didn't stop. All the way to Auschwitz. We didn't know then where we were going. We-- we assumed we were going for labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were in a ghetto first.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the ghetto for about four to five weeks. I don't exactly know how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that ghetto was in which town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dragomiresti. It was called in Yiddish, it's Dragomiresti. In Hungarian-- forget now which Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was with you and your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- all along. All along. We got on the train. It was all the families. Anything from 150 people upwards. And we were together with the families. And I remember afterwards thinking about it later on, that we-- my mother, they would hardly give us anything to eat for all these two, two and a half days we were traveling. Because they were saying, we don't know when we're going to be able to bake bread, when we be able to do some cooking. So they would rationing. They-- we had quite a lot of food. But they would-- they would-- they-- they reserved it for we don't know when we going to be able to bake and all the rest. And really and truly, when we got to Auschwitz, it was-- the-- the doors of the trains opened. Everybody out. And leave your luggage. Everything will be taken to your rooms, and all the rest of it. So it's-- it's hurt to me even now to think that-- that the parents were thinking of, obviously, for the best. But that's how we were deceived. Because, you know.
